anchor.snippet: Chaos theory is an interdisciplinary area of scientific study and branch of mathematics
The butterfly effect , an underlying principle of chaos, describes how a small change in one state of a deterministic nonlinear system can result in large differences in a later state
This behavior is known as deterministic chaos , or simply chaos .
When the present determines the future but the approximate present does not approximately determine the future

annotations.label: On intervals transitivity implies the rest

anchor.snippet: If attention is restricted to intervals , the second property implies the other two
An alternative and a generally weaker definition of chaos uses only the first two properties
Sensitivity to initial conditions means that each point in a chaotic system is arbitrarily closely approximated by other points

Topological mixing (or the weaker condition of topological transitivity) means that the system evolves over time so that any given region or open set of its phase space eventually overlaps with any other given region
consider the simple dynamical system produced by repeatedly doubling an initial value

The Birkhoff Transitivity Theorem states that if X is a second countable
the existence of a dense orbit implies topological transitivity
For a chaotic system to have dense periodic orbits means that every point in the space is approached arbitrarily closely by periodic orbits

any continuous one-dimensional system that exhibits a regular cycle of period three will also display regular cycles of every other length
The cases of most interest arise when the chaotic behavior takes place on an attractor
the attractors that arise from chaotic systems, known as strange attractors , have great detail and complexity

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: Finite-dimensional linear systems are never chaotic; for a dynamical system to display chaotic behavior, it must be either nonlinear or infinite-dimensional

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem shows that a strange attractor can only arise in three or more dimensions

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: three-dimensional quadratic systems with only three or four terms on the right-hand side cannot exhibit chaotic behavior
